title: Practice Test: Can this medieval Byzantine shipwreck rewrite history? / Everything you need to know about Rocky Mountain National Park / When and where to see the best cherry blossoms in the U.S.

passages.title: Can this medieval Byzantine shipwreck rewrite history?
passages.sourceTitle: Can this medieval Byzantine shipwreck rewrite history?
passages.contentHtml:
A More than a thousand years after a ship vanished off the coast of modern-day Croatia, archaeologists have uncovered a wreck that might reshape our ideas of the medieval world.

B The sight of a large bronze medieval cauldron lying on the seabed among some moldering old ship’s timbers first piqued Igor Miholjek’s interest.

C The presence of the old cauldron, together with the scatter of amphorae, suggested that the lost vessel was a Byzantine ship, an extremely rare find in the Adriatic Sea.

D Today, after a decade of painstaking progress, Miholjek and his team have uncovered a rich trove of artifacts that has opened a valuable window into travel and trade in the Adriatic.

E The search to answer such tantalizing questions is beginning to give historians a glimpse into an empire facing tumultuous times.

F Despite the potential trove of underwater evidence, excavating the Byzantine wreck was not for the faint of heart.

G Shipwrecks are time capsules, snapshots of life precisely as it was on a particular day.

passages.title: Everything you need to know about Rocky Mountain National Park
passages.sourceTitle: Everything you need to know about Rocky Mountain National Park
passages.contentHtml:
A Colorado’s iconic park protects wind-sculpted tundra, summer wildflower meadows, and enduring Indigenous presence across one of the nation’s largest continuous mountain landscapes.

B Few places in the United States deliver such immediate access to high-altitude wilderness as Rocky Mountain National Park.

C In the park’s foreground, 14,259-foot Longs Peak anchors a jagged skyline of cirques, snowfields, and ridgelines carved by ancient ice.

D Summer brings wildflowers, snow-free trails, and golden aspen foliage, but also teeming crowds.

E Higher still, Trail Ridge Road—America’s highest continuous paved highway—is Rocky’s grandest stage.

F The Alpine Visitor Center at 11,796 feet, with interpretive exhibits and stunning views in every direction, marks the beginning of the westward descent of the Divide.

G Rocky Mountain’s 6,700-foot elevation range stacks tundra, forest, meadow, and willow flats into wildlife-rich habitat zones.

passages.title: When and where to see the best cherry blossoms in the U.S.
passages.sourceTitle: When and where to see the best cherry blossoms in the U.S.
passages.contentHtml:
A Since the introduction of cherry trees from their native Japan in the early 1900s, blossoming cherry trees signal seasonal change in the United States.

B The tradition of cherry blossom festivals in the U.S. dates to 1935 in Washington, D.C.

C Not only do these celebrations in communities around the country persist as symbols of international peace and friendship, but they also mirror hanami in Japan.

D An individual cherry tree blooms for about a week, while whole groves bloom and fall in about two.

E The National Park Service, tasked with predicting peak bloom in advance of the capital city’s National Cherry Blossom Festival, says it’s virtually impossible to say with accuracy more than 10 days in advance.

F To make your own educated guess on when cherry trees will be in full bloom, check historical peak bloom times in D.C. or watch the “Bloom Cam.”

G The Dallas Arboretum hosts a weeks-long celebration of spring featuring concerts, talks, floral arrangement classes, beer and wine pairings, and outdoor picnics.
